title: Harvesting angora fibre
subtitle: How to clip or pluck an angora rabbit and prepare the fleece for spinning.
excerpt: Angora rabbits produce fibre as a by-product of their normal coat cycle. Harvesting it every 90 days prevents matting and wool block, and gives you the raw material for one of the finest spinning fibres available from any domestic animal.
sourceNotes: British Angora Goat Society notes on fibre production (cross-reference for fibre handling). BFBA (British Fancy Rabbit Clubs), angora breed care guidelines. Spinner's and Weaver's Annual, angora fibre preparation notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A serious and often fatal condition in angora rabbits in which swallowed fibre accumulates in the stomach and cannot pass through the digestive tract. More common when the rabbit is moulting and swallows loose fibre while grooming. Regular harvesting and daily brushing during moult are the primary preventions.

Angora fibre harvested at the optimal length of 7 to 12 cm, before the moult causes the fibre to matt or felt at the base. Prime fleece has intact staples and maximum length. Fibre harvested too late or from a matted coat loses significant value and spinning quality.

content.text: Angora rabbits are harvested every 90 days. Leaving the coat longer risks matting and
from swallowed fibre. The harvest coincides roughly with the natural moult cycle. A 90-day schedule gives a
with the best fibre length for spinning.

English and French angora rabbits have a moult cycle that allows hand-plucking; loose fibres come away with gentle pulling when the rabbit is in moult. Plucked fibre has a tapered tip that spins with a natural lustre. German and Giant angora breeds do not moult in the same way and are always clipped with scissors or clippers. If the fibre does not come away easily with gentle pulling, clip rather than pull.

Place the rabbit on a non-slip surface or hold it on your lap. Work from the back forward, lifting small sections of fibre and cutting parallel to the skin with sharp scissors. Keep the scissor blade flat against the body to avoid cutting the skin. Leave at least 2 to 3 cm of fibre on the rabbit to protect it from temperature extremes. Clip the belly last; the belly fibre is shorter and the skin there is thin and easily nicked.

Collect the fibre into a paper bag or a breathable bag as you go, keeping the prime body fibre separate from the shorter belly and leg fibre. Do not compress the fibre into a ball; this causes felting. If the fibre is damp from the rabbit grooming, spread it on a clean surface and let it dry before storing. Store in paper bags or loosely in a box in a dry location.

A freshly clipped angora rabbit will feel the cold more than usual. Do not clip in cold weather if the rabbit is housed outdoors, or provide additional bedding depth immediately after harvesting. Check for any skin nicks and apply a small amount of antiseptic if needed. Increase hay provision for 48 hours after a full clip to encourage gut motility, as the rabbit may have ingested loose fibre during the session.
